claim: Fork accounts for ~28-32% of all tactical opportunities in chess
notes: Convergence across three independent datasets

claim: Pin accounts for ~18-22% of tactical opportunities
notes: Some sources rank pin higher than fork depending on classification

claim: Skewer accounts for ~10-14% of tactical opportunities
notes: Less data available than fork/pin

claim: The 'Big Four' (fork, pin, skewer, discovered attack) account for ~70-80% of tactical opportunities below 2000 Elo
notes: Cross-validated by frequency and user reports

claim: Learning one pattern reduces installation time of synergistic patterns by 30-50%
notes: From CHREST simulations; not directly tested in humans

claim: CHREST chunk installation requires ~8-10 seconds per chunk
notes: Robust across multiple experimental paradigms

claim: Template formation requires ~60 seconds
notes: From 'Five seconds or sixty?' paper

claim: Experts store 10,000-100,000 chunks in long-term memory
notes: Range from Gilmartin & Simon; widely cited

claim: Fork-trained networks install pin templates 34% faster
notes: Single simulation study; exact percentage may vary

claim: Removal of defender appears in 40-60% of multi-move combinations
notes: Pedagogical claim; underlying methodology not fully documented

claim: Capturing defender accuracy jumps +14.7 percentage points via woodpecker cycles
notes: Disco Chess data from 431,899 puzzle attempts

claim: Pin accuracy improves only +1.0pp via woodpecker cycles
notes: Disco Chess data; significant difference from capturing defender

claim: Smothered mate has highest baseline accuracy (98.2%) across Disco Chess users
notes: Easily recognizable pattern once learned

claim: Theme accuracy ranges from 76.2% (capturing defender) to 98.2% (smothered mate)
notes: Across all Disco Chess users

claim: Transitional problems / easy-to-hard curriculum outperforms random or hard-first in chess
notes: 2026 'Level Up' paper; tested with neural networks on chess puzzles

claim: Fork peaks at 800-900 rating band on Lichess, deflection at ~1400, trapped piece at ~1600
notes: Lichess puzzle database analysis by SayChessClassical

claim: Consistent Disco Chess trainers gain average +35 Elo
notes: Self-selected user sample; not a controlled experiment

claim: Top 15% of Disco Chess users gain over +100 Elo in under a month
notes: Platform claims without independent verification

claim: STLCC curriculum sequences patterns as: Fork (Level 2), Pin (Level 3), Skewer and Discovered Attack (Level 4), Back-Rank and Removal of Defender (Level 5)
notes: Publicly available curriculum guide from Saint Louis Chess Club

claim: Users who spread training over time gained +6.0pp accuracy vs +2.8pp for cramming
notes: Disco Chess analytics; correlation not necessarily causation

claim: Fork decides roughly 15% of games rated 800-1400
notes: Estimates from chess.lc and forum user consensus

claim: Efficiency improves from 1.00x (cycle 1) to 4.46x (cycle 7)
notes: Disco Chess data across 65 users, 157,751 solves

claim: The Woodpecker Method helped Hans Tikkanen achieve 3 GM norms in 7 weeks
notes: Well-documented achievement in chess training literature

claim: By cycle 2, woodpecker users solve puzzles 24% faster at higher accuracy
notes: Disco Chess aggregated data

claim: CQL scan of 372,000 GM games shows fork as most common opening tactic motif
notes: Lindemann's GitHub analysis with published CQL scripts
